question: When should I use a histogram versus a density plot for one numeric variable?
reference_answer: For one numeric variable, use a histogram or density plot to show the distribution. Histograms bin the data; density plots smooth it.

question: What chart types work for two numeric variables?
reference_answer: For two numeric variables, use scatter plots to show relationships. If x is time-ordered, prefer a line chart. For many points, use alpha, hexbin, or 2D density.

question: How should I compare groups when I have one categorical and one numeric variable?
reference_answer: Use bar/column charts (geom_col), ordered by value when rank matters. For distribution within groups, use boxplot or violin with x = category.

question: When should I use facet_wrap or facet_grid?
reference_answer: Use facet_wrap or facet_grid when the same chart type repeats per region, sex, year, or other subgroup — small multiples beat overloaded single panels.

question: Why avoid pie charts for precise comparison?
reference_answer: Pie charts work only with few slices; prefer bar charts for precise comparison between categories.

question: What does a data_to_viz filename like 7_OneCatOneNum mean?
reference_answer: Filenames like 7_OneCatOneNum.csv mean one categorical plus one numeric variable — start with bar, boxplot, or ordered dot plot.

question: What FT-inspired principle applies to chart titles?
reference_answer: Use a headline-style title stating the takeaway; subtitle gives scope, time range, units, or caveats. The chart proves what the title claims.

question: How should I label data sources in ggplot2 following FT style?
reference_answer: Use labs(title, subtitle, caption) with source and notes in caption, not buried in body text.

question: What accessibility guidance applies to color in charts?
reference_answer: Do not rely on color alone; use position, labels, or pattern. Check palettes for colorblind safety (viridis, Okabe-Ito).

question: What is the VAD framework task abstraction?
reference_answer: Task abstraction asks why you are visualizing: common tasks include lookup (single value), compare (which is larger), identify (outliers), and summarize (distribution or trend). Match chart to task.

question: What marks and channels does VAD recommend for magnitude?
reference_answer: Marks include points, lines, areas, bars, text. Channels include position (strongest), length, angle, area, color. Prefer position and length for magnitude.

question: How do I handle many categories on a bar chart?
reference_answer: Use horizontal bars (coord_flip) or show top-N plus Other when there are many categories.

question: When plotting rank data on the y axis, what scale trick helps?
reference_answer: Lower rank means more popular; consider scale_y_reverse() when rank is on the y axis.

question: What ggplot2 theme is recommended for clean FT-style charts?
reference_answer: theme_minimal() or a restrained custom theme; avoid chartjunk. Use scale_* with readable breaks like scales::comma.

question: When should I truncate the y-axis?
reference_answer: Truncate y-axis only when the baseline is meaningful and distortion is obvious; default to zero for counts.

question: What data abstraction types does VAD describe?
reference_answer: VAD data types include tables (items + attributes), networks (nodes + links), spatial (geometry + attributes), and fields (continuous samples).

question: How should I plot time series trends in ggplot2?
reference_answer: Use geom_line for continuous trends; points for sparse years. Highlight one series with color or size if the story focuses on one group.

question: What is wrong with dual y-axis charts for general audiences?
reference_answer: Avoid dual y-axis unless the audience is expert; prefer two panels instead.

question: How do small multiples relate to FT chart principles?
reference_answer: Small multiples beat overloaded single panels when comparing regions or groups; one strong message per chart.

question: Why are facet_grid bar chart name labels overlapping on the x-axis?
reference_answer: facet_grid shares axis levels across panels by default. Use aes(x = Number, y = Name) with facet_grid(..., scales = free) so each panel gets its own name list and count range; reorder with fct_reorder inside group_by. A shared count axis makes small regions invisible when counts differ by orders of magnitude.

question: What VAD validation question should I ask before publishing a chart?
reference_answer: Ask whether the chart answers the stated task, whether a table would be clearer, and whether a reader can state the takeaway in one sentence from the title.
